author: dunkin-novice
done: Added a protocol === 'capacitor:' early return in isLocalDevHost() in js/asset-config.js. The function previously matched on hostname only ('localhost' / '127.0.0.1' / '[::1]'), which collides with capacitor://localhost — Capacitor's WKWebView scheme. The collision routed Capacitor through the dev-revision branch (dev-${Date.now()}), busting the immutable CDN cache on every reload. Guarding inside isLocalDevHost means any future caller of that helper also benefits, not just getDefaultAssetRevision.
fix: Early-return false in isLocalDevHost when window.location.protocol === 'capacitor:'.
goal: Stop iOS Simulator from redownloading every card art image on every reload once the Capacitor wrap lands. Pre-emptive fix during Track 1.11 scaffold.
notes: Lands directly on main, no feature branch — surgical 4-line guard with non-controversial scope. Companion to Track 1.11 Capacitor scaffold (separate branch). Has zero runtime effect on the live site (production is https://, http://localhost is unaffected); only matters once a Capacitor wrap exists.
root_cause: isLocalDevHost identified Capacitor's WebView scheme as a dev environment because both share the 'localhost' hostname. Protocol was the discriminator we never checked.
verified: node --test passes 64/65 — the one failing test (tests/poster-debug-ci.test.js) is environmental (requires playwright in node_modules; pre-existing failure unrelated to this change). No other test references asset-config.

filed: Capacitor wrap dual-tree sync: js/vendor/supabase.js + updated js/auth.js must be synced to www/ and ios/App/App/public/ via the sync:cap workflow before next iOS build
Verify Cloudflare auto-gzips js/vendor/supabase.js — 695KB raw should compress to ~170KB on the wire; check _headers if it doesn't
Debug commit ec7efa7 remains in git history — content reverted in this patch but the commit itself is not removed
fix: Vendor @supabase/supabase-js@2.105.4 as a self-contained ESM bundle at js/vendor/supabase.js (695KB raw via esbuild). Switched the dynamic import target in js/auth.js from the esm.sh URL to ./vendor/supabase.js. Also stripped temporary debug console.log lines from js/ladder-b.js that were shipped in commit ec7efa7.
goal: Fix Safari iOS+desktop breaking all Supabase auth flows due to dynamic import of esm.sh module
notes: Rebundle command for future supabase-js updates: npx esbuild ./node_modules/@supabase/supabase-js --bundle --format=esm --outfile=js/vendor/supabase.js — note the leading ./ is required; esbuild interprets bare paths as package names. package-lock.json committed for the first time in repo history per founder decision.
root_cause: Dynamic ESM import of a remote CDN URL (esm.sh) is unreliable in Safari — the module either fails to parse, fails CORS preflight, or hangs depending on iOS/desktop version. Chrome/Firefox tolerate it; Safari does not.
symptom: On Safari iOS and Safari desktop, await import('https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2') fails, so the client never instantiates and every authed surface silently degrades (login, reading history sync, Ladder B).

done: Added resetActiveDeck() in js/data.js (3-line export that sets module-level activeDeckId back to DEFAULT_DECK_ID). Extended js/auth.js onAuthStateChange listener to call resetActiveDeck() when _event === 'SIGNED_OUT'. Extended named import on js/auth.js:2 to include resetActiveDeck. root_cause: Module-level cache in js/data.js — `export let activeDeckId = getActiveDeckId();` runs once at module load. getActiveDeckId() correctly re-reads cleared localStorage after logout and returns 'moonmallow', but getActiveDeck() reads the cached `activeDeckId` variable, not localStorage. symptom: After SPA-style logout (no hard reload), deck-inventory re-renders and shows the previously-selected streak-locked deck (e.g. boba-oracle) as active even though the user has no session. Hard reload self-heals the bug

author: dunkin-novice
fix: Three functional edits in js/login-reward.js: (a) added setActiveDeck to the existing import from ./data.js so the function is in scope; (b) changed the popup-build deckBackUrl reference from DECKS.moonmallow.backImage to DECKS['veila-tarot'].backImage so the modal shows Veila's card back; (c) added setActiveDeck('veila-tarot') as the first line of the CTA click handler, before markSeen() and dismiss(). Also updated the file-header docstring (line 5) to reference Veila Tarot instead of Moonmallow and to note that the CTA grants the deck. canUnlockDeck('veila-tarot') already returns true once a user is signed in (deck has role: 'default' in js/data.js line 24), so setActiveDeck succeeds without further changes.
goal: Grant the veila-tarot deck on first Google sign-in. The login-reward popup was supposed to do this on its CTA, but the grant action did not exist anywhere in the codebase.
notes: The per-device flag pattern (localStorage 'meowtarot_login_reward_seen') means this fix lands only on devices that have not yet seen the popup. Devices where the previous broken CTA was already clicked carry seen=true and will never see the corrected popup unless localStorage is cleared. If that matters, a follow-up could bump the storage key (e.g. meowtarot_login_reward_seen_v2) to re-fire for everyone. Not done in this commit because it would also re-fire for users who legitimately dismissed the old popup. Flag retained for product decision.
root_cause: Two defects in js/login-reward.js: (1) the deckBackUrl computed at the popup-build site referenced DECKS.moonmallow.backImage, so the modal showed the starter deck art instead of the deck being granted; (2) the CTA click handler called only markSeen() + dismiss() — no setActiveDeck(), no grant call, nothing that would actually transfer veila-tarot to the user. Grepping js/ for grantDeck, unlockDeck, ownedDecks, or any veila reference confirmed there is no per-user ownership model in the code; the only mechanism that would land veila on the user is setActiveDeck('veila-tarot'), which was never invoked from the CTA. Detection chain via auth.js onAuthStateChange → maybeShowLoginReward was wired correctly; only the grant step inside the CTA was missing.
symptom: Users completing their first Google OAuth sign-in saw the welcome popup, clicked the CTA, the popup dismissed — and nothing else happened. Veila Tarot did not become the active deck. The popup itself also advertised the wrong deck art (Moonmallow card back instead of Veila).
